resume:
DEVAN OKONKWO
Aspiring Sales Development Representative | Rising Senior, B.B.A. Marketing
Campus: Lakeshore State University, College of Business | Expected graduation: May next year | GPA 3.6/4.0

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Highly motivated, coachable, and relentlessly customer-obsessed business student seeking a summer Sales Development Representative (SDR) internship where I can apply a consultative, value-first selling philosophy to a high-velocity inbound and outbound motion. Over the past three years I have built a foundation in modern B2B sales fundamentals through coursework, two part-time customer-facing roles, a competitive sales case program, and self-directed study of frameworks such as MEDDIC, SPIN Selling, Challenger, and the Sandler system. I am comfortable with activity metrics, pipeline hygiene, and CRM discipline, and I genuinely enjoy the daily grind of prospecting, personalizing outreach, and booking qualified meetings. My goal for this internship is to learn what world-class top-of-funnel execution actually looks like inside a real revenue org, contribute meaningfully to pipeline generation, and earn a return offer.

EDUCATION
Lakeshore State University — Bachelor of Business Administration, Marketing concentration, Sales minor
- Relevant coursework: Professional Selling, Sales Force Management, Consumer Behavior, Business Analytics, Negotiation & Conflict Resolution, Marketing Research, Database Concepts (intro SQL)
- GPA 3.6; Dean's List three semesters
- Sales minor capstone in progress: a semester-long territory plan and live role-play assessment graded by industry judges

SALES & CUSTOMER-FACING EXPERIENCE
Lakeshore Telefund — Student Caller / Team Lead, two academic years
- Conducted outbound calls to university alumni for the annual giving campaign, working from a dialer and a donor CRM with detailed call dispositions
- Personally averaged roughly the top tier of the calling floor for pledges per shift, though I did not track my exact conversion percentage at the time
- Promoted to Team Lead in my second year; coached a pod of six new callers on opening scripts, objection handling, and tone
- Helped refresh the rebuttal guide used by the floor after noticing that a common 'I already gave' objection was ending calls early
- Learned to handle rejection at volume, to personalize an ask to a donor's graduation year and college, and to log clean notes so the next caller had context

Brightline Outdoor Gear — Retail Sales Associate (seasonal, two summers)
- Sold technical outdoor equipment (tents, packs, footwear) in a consultative, needs-discovery style rather than transactional ringing-up
- Consistently recognized by store leadership for attach rate on accessories and for converting browsers into multi-item purchases
- Trained two incoming seasonal associates on the product ladder and on asking open-ended discovery questions before recommending gear
- Handled returns and service recovery, turning several frustrated customers into repeat buyers through follow-up

LEADERSHIP & COMPETITIVE SALES
National Collegiate Sales Competition (NCSC) — University Team Member
- Selected through an internal tryout to represent the university in a national role-play selling competition
- Advanced past the first round in a simulated discovery-and-demo call judged by sales executives from sponsoring companies
- Spent roughly eight weeks in structured practice: recorded role-plays, peer feedback, and tape review with a faculty coach
- Studied buyer personas, qualification frameworks, and how to surface pain, budget, and decision criteria inside a single 12-minute call

Pi Sigma Epsilon (professional sales & marketing fraternity) — VP of Professional Development
- Organized a speaker series bringing in working SDRs, account executives, and a regional sales manager to talk about quota, ramp, and the realities of the job
- Coordinated a mock-interview night and a LinkedIn-prospecting workshop for chapter members

PROJECTS & SELF-DIRECTED LEARNING
Territory Plan Capstone (in progress)
- Building a hypothetical territory plan for a mid-market SaaS product: ideal customer profile, account tiering, outreach cadence, and a 90-day activity model
- Modeling the math of top-of-funnel: how many touches and connects it takes to book a meeting, and how meetings convert to opportunities
Personal study
- Read and took notes on several core sales books and completed a free online prospecting course; practiced writing cold-email sequences and personalized LinkedIn messages on my own time

TOOLS & SKILLS
- CRM exposure: donor CRM at Telefund; self-taught basics of a popular SaaS CRM through a free training portal
- Comfortable with spreadsheets for tracking activity; introductory SQL from coursework
- Outreach: cold calling, cold email, LinkedIn social selling, objection handling
- Frameworks studied: MEDDIC, SPIN, Challenger, Sandler, BANT
- Soft skills: coachability, resilience, active listening, time management across a full course load and part-time work

WHAT I'M LOOKING FOR
I want to plug into a team with a real playbook and strong sales leadership so I can absorb how disciplined prospecting and qualification are done at scale. I am eager to be held to activity and outcome metrics, to get tape reviewed, and to iterate fast. I bring energy, a strong work ethic, genuine curiosity about buyers, and a track record of being the person who volunteers to make the extra calls.

REFERENCES
Faculty sales coach and Telefund supervisor available on request.
jd: We are hiring a summer Sales Development Representative (SDR) intern for our B2B SaaS revenue team. You will sit on the outbound pod, working a defined account list under a senior SDR mentor and the SDR manager. Day to day you will research accounts, write and send personalized cold email and LinkedIn sequences, make cold calls, and book qualified discovery meetings for Account Executives. You will be held to clear weekly activity targets (calls, connects, sequences) and a meetings-booked goal that ramps over the summer. We care less about prior closing experience and more about coachability, work ethic, and whether you can internalize and execute a playbook. You will get your call recordings reviewed, learn our qualification framework, and be expected to keep the CRM clean. A return full-time offer is on the table for strong performers. Required: relentless follow-through, comfort being measured by numbers, and the ability to take direct feedback and change behavior quickly.

history.q: Tell me about a time you had to do high-volume outreach or calling. What was the role and what did the day actually look like?
At Telefund you say you were 'top tier' on the floor. How were callers ranked, and where did you actually land?
You mentioned refreshing the rebuttal guide. What was the specific objection, what did you change the script to, and did calls actually go differently after?
Forget frameworks for a second. Walk me through one real call where a donor said no and you turned it around — what did you actually say?
In the NCSC role-play, what was the single piece of feedback your coach gave you that changed how you sell?
Last one on this: this internship is measured on meetings booked. If your connect-to-meeting rate was low in week three, what would you change first, and why that?

candidate_last_answer: Great question. So my philosophy is that selling is really about being relentlessly customer-obsessed and leading with value, right? If my connect-to-meeting rate was lagging, the first thing I'd do is double-click on the fundamentals and make sure I'm running a truly consultative, value-first motion at the top of the funnel. I'd want to align on the north star, leverage my activity metrics, and really lean into MEDDIC to qualify hard and book only high-intent meetings. At the end of the day it's about working smarter, owning my pipeline, and bringing my A-game every single day. I'm a big believer in the daily grind and just out-hustling everyone, so I'd grind harder and trust the process.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'd want to align on the north star, leverage my activity metrics, and really lean into MEDDIC

expected-yield: strip away the rehearsed buzzwords and force one concrete diagnostic step — what specific part of the call (opener, list quality, talk track, time-of-day) he would inspect first when connect-to-meeting is low, how he would tell which one is the real bottleneck, and what change he would test, demonstrating actual mechanism over slogans
